title: Teaching
text:
My teaching focuses on helping students use geospatial tools to solve real problems, communicate clearly, and build professional confidence. I teach GIS, cartography, uncrewed aerial systems, remote sensing, spatial analysis, and professional-development topics through applied projects and realistic workflows.
Rather than treating GIS as a collection of disconnected tools, I try to help students understand the full problem-solving process: defining the question, identifying useful data, evaluating limitations, choosing appropriate methods, producing clear deliverables, and explaining results to an audience.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Areas

Geographic Information Systems: Applied GIS workflows, spatial analysis, data management, and problem solving.
Cartography and Geovisualization: Map design, visual communication, audience awareness, and effective geospatial storytelling.
Uncrewed Aerial Systems: FAA Part 107 concepts, flight planning, data collection, photogrammetry, safety, and applied UAV workflows.
Remote Sensing: Image interpretation, sensor characteristics, data products, and applications.
Professional Preparation: Portfolio development, conference preparation, project communication, career pathways, and professional expectations.

title: Teaching
text:
Teaching Approach
I design courses and labs around realistic scenarios. Students are often asked to work through problems similar to those they might encounter in professional practice: identifying what data are needed, determining whether the data are appropriate, choosing tools and methods, producing deliverables, and communicating results clearly.
This approach helps students see GIS as a process rather than a button-clicking exercise. The goal is not simply to complete a tutorial, but to understand how geospatial analysis supports decision-making.

title: Public Safety Proximity Analysis
text:
The Public Safety Proximity Analysis lab is an applied GIS assignment developed for GEOG 311: Introduction to Geovisualization and GIS.
The lab, titled Clandestine Meth Labs, asks students to use GIS tools to investigate proximity relationships between former clandestine meth lab locations and schools in Tennessee. The activity emphasizes practical GIS problem-solving with imperfect real-world data, including data conversion, geocoding, feature creation, proximity analysis, and table-based summaries.

title: Public Safety Proximity Analysis
text:
Teaching Context
This lab gives students experience with a realistic GIS workflow where the desired data are not already packaged in an ideal format. Students begin with public records in a PDF, convert those records into a usable table, geocode locations, create GIS features, and conduct spatial analysis to answer specific public-safety questions.
The activity is designed to help students understand that real GIS work often begins before the first map is made. Students must organize data, convert formats, create usable spatial layers, evaluate proximity relationships, and summarize results in a lab report.

title: Public Safety Proximity Analysis
text:
Guiding Questions
This lab asks students to consider:

How close are things together?
How many features are located inside a particular area?
What should I do when the data I need are not available in a GIS-ready format?
How can I convert tabular locations into spatial data?
How can I convert points to polygons to support choropleth mapping?
How can proximity tools help answer applied public-safety questions?

title: Public Safety Proximity Analysis
text:

title: Public Safety Proximity Analysis
text:
Student Deliverable
Students complete a lab report that uses GIS analysis to answer applied questions, including:

How many schools in Anderson, Blount, and Knox counties are located within 0.30 miles of a former clandestine meth lab?
Which school in Tennessee is located nearest to a former clandestine meth lab?

title: Public Safety Proximity Analysis
text:
Teaching Significance
This example reflects my approach to teaching GIS as a practical problem-solving process. The lab requires students to move from messy source data to usable spatial data, then apply multiple GIS tools to answer specific analytical questions.
It also helps students see that spatial analysis depends on a chain of decisions: how data are obtained, how locations are created, how features are selected, how distances are measured, and how results are summarized. By completing the lab report, students practice not only GIS operations, but also explaining the analytical process and interpreting results clearly.

title: In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities
text:
The In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities were developed as part of a shift from a traditional synchronous lecture format to a flipped course model. After moving lectures to pre-recorded videos, in-class time could be used for short, focused practice activities that helped students build GIS skills before attempting larger lab assignments.
These activities were designed in response to a teaching challenge I observed in introductory GIS courses: students were often intimidated by full lab assignments, especially when they were still developing confidence with GIS concepts, software interfaces, data organization, and spatial-analysis workflows.

title: In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities
text:
Teaching Context
These activities were developed for GEOG 311: Introduction to Geovisualization and GIS. They were intentionally short, low-stakes, and focused on specific skills or concepts that students would later use in larger assignments.
The goal was to make in-class time more active and supportive. Instead of using class meetings primarily for lecture, students could practice, ask questions, make mistakes, troubleshoot, and build confidence with guidance.

title: In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities
text:
Project Role
My role included designing the in-class activities, aligning them with larger lab assignments, creating supporting instructions, selecting appropriate datasets and examples, and using class time to help students practice core GIS skills.
The activity instructions are maintained as Google Docs for classroom use. Public links may be added later after reviewing the materials for data permissions, student-facing scaffolding, and course-context details.

title: In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities
text:
Instructional Goals
These activities were designed to help students:

build confidence before larger lab assignments,
practice GIS skills in a low-stakes environment,
connect lecture concepts to hands-on work,
become more comfortable with GIS software and web GIS tools,
improve data organization habits,
develop spatial-thinking and interpretation skills,
ask better questions before working independently.

title: In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities
text:
Selected Activity Examples

Data Organization Exercise



Screenshot from the Data Organization Exercise.


A foundational activity focused on file management, workspace organization, data formats, and the practical habits students need for successful GIS work. Students download public datasets from the USGS Earthquake Catalog and the NYC Open Data Portal, practice working with CSV and shapefile downloads, unzip data, and organize files into a structured workspace.
This activity helps students understand that GIS work depends on more than software tools. Before students can map or analyze data, they need to know how to download, store, unzip, identify, and manage geospatial files.
Skills and tools: USGS Earthquake Catalog, NYC Open Data, CSV files, shapefiles, ZIP extraction, folder naming, workspace setup, data organization, cross-platform file handling.


Data Exploration Exercise with ArcGIS Online



Screenshot from the Data Exploration Exercise with ArcGIS Online.


An introductory web GIS activity that helps students explore, visualize, and interact with earthquake data in ArcGIS Online. Students upload a CSV from the USGS Earthquake Catalog, visualize the data as points, experiment with symbol size and color, change basemaps, explore pop-ups, inspect attribute tables, and save a web map.
The activity is designed to give students an early success with real geospatial data. It introduces the idea that a table containing latitude and longitude coordinates can become an interactive map and that visualization choices affect how patterns are interpreted.
Skills and tools: ArcGIS Online, Map Viewer Classic, CSV upload, web maps, basemaps, pop-ups, attribute tables, symbolization by magnitude and depth, point size, point color, exploratory data visualization.


In-Class Web GIS Exercise Using Kepler.gl



Screenshot from the In-Class Web GIS Exercise Using Kepler.gl.


A web mapping activity that introduces students to rapid visualization using an open-source mapping platform. Students download earthquake data from the USGS and squirrel census data from NYC Open Data, then use Kepler.gl to create and compare different map types, including points, grids, hexbins, clusters, and heat maps.
This activity helps students compare visualization methods and think about which techniques best communicate different kinds of spatial patterns. It also gives students experience working outside the Esri ecosystem while reinforcing core web GIS concepts.
Skills and tools: Kepler.gl, USGS Earthquake Catalog, NYC Open Data, CSV, GeoJSON, web mapping, filtering, attribute-based styling, point maps, grid maps, hexbins, clusters, heat maps, 3D visualization.


Basic Vector Tools In-Class Activity



Screenshot from the Basic Vector Tools In-Class Activity.


A low-stakes ArcGIS Pro practice activity that helps students become familiar with common vector geoprocessing tools before applying them in larger lab assignments. Students work through short examples using Tennessee tornado tracks, Lower Mississippi watershed boundaries, USDA plant hardiness zones, and Tennessee wildfire perimeters.
The activity gives students guided practice with common GIS operations while also reinforcing data organization, spatial reference awareness, attribute-table interpretation, and tool-output comparison.
Skills and tools: ArcGIS Pro, geodatabases, buffer by field, dissolve, clip, merge, attribute-table review, spatial-reference interpretation, public GIS datasets, data organization.


In-Class Intermediate Vector Tools Practice



Screenshot from the In-Class Intermediate Vector Tools Practice.


An intermediate ArcGIS Pro activity that gives students guided practice with a wider range of vector workflows. Students create point features from latitude and longitude fields, create line features from start and end coordinates, repair missing spatial-reference information, generate random points, create service areas, calculate minimum bounding geometries, generate tessellations, and summarize bike-rack capacity using spatial joins.
The activity is intentionally broad, giving students repeated exposure to different geoprocessing patterns before they are expected to use these tools more independently. It also helps students recognize that GIS tools often support different parts of a larger analytical workflow.
Skills and tools: ArcGIS Pro, geodatabases, XY to Point, XY to Line, Define Projection, Create Random Points, Generate Service Areas, Minimum Bounding Geometry, Generate Tessellation, Spatial Join, coordinate fields, spatial references, service areas, hexagon aggregation.


In-Class Intermediate Vector Tools Practice 2: Working With Tables



Screenshot from the Working With Tables activity.


An intermediate activity focused on table-based GIS reasoning, selections, summaries, and proximity analysis. Students use tornado data to practice definition queries, summary statistics, and chart creation, then revisit a previous meth lab proximity question using online feature services, selected-feature workflows, and Select by Location.
This activity helps students move beyond “make a map” thinking and toward evidence-based GIS analysis. It emphasizes that many GIS questions are answered through attribute tables, filters, selections, summaries, and careful interpretation of selected records.
Skills and tools: ArcGIS Pro, definition queries, summary statistics, charts, attribute tables, Select by Location, Add Data from Path, online feature services, selected-feature workflows, proximity analysis, table interpretation.


Point Pattern Practice with SoHo Cholera Outbreak



Screenshot from the SoHo Cholera point pattern activity.


A point-pattern analysis activity built around the famous 1854 cholera outbreak in the SoHo neighborhood of London. Students take on the role of Dr. John Snow and use GIS techniques to investigate whether cholera deaths appear to be associated with a contaminated water pump.
The activity asks students to compare multiple visualization and analysis techniques, including graduated color, proportional symbols, buffers, Thiessen polygons, kernel density, and hot spot analysis. Students then decide which technique would be most effective for communicating evidence to a non-technical audience.
This activity is longer and more ambitious than the other in-class exercises, but it is valuable because it connects GIS analysis, epidemiology, spatial reasoning, historical data, and public communication.
Skills and tools: ArcGIS Online, graduated color, proportional symbols, buffers, Aggregate Points, Thiessen polygons, kernel density, hot spot analysis, point-pattern analysis, georeferencing concepts, heads-up digitizing concepts, spatial evidence, public-facing map communication.

title: In-Class GIS Skill-Building Activities
text:
Teaching Significance
This collection reflects my approach to teaching GIS through scaffolding, practice, and confidence-building. Rather than expecting students to jump directly from lecture to complex labs, these activities create intermediate steps where students can practice specific skills, receive support, and build readiness for more independent work.
The activities also show how a flipped classroom model can make GIS instruction more active. Pre-recorded lectures provide conceptual background, while class time becomes a space for applied practice, troubleshooting, and skill development.
Taken together, these activities show a progression from foundational data management and web mapping to vector geoprocessing, table-based analysis, and point-pattern interpretation. The emphasis is not only on using GIS tools, but on helping students understand when and why those tools are useful.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
The Applied GIS Lab Design approach reflects my effort to teach GIS as a complete problem-solving process rather than a sequence of disconnected tools.
In many GIS courses, students learn individual software operations without always understanding how those operations fit into a larger workflow. My goal is to design labs that begin with realistic questions, require students to identify and prepare appropriate data, guide them through analysis and interpretation, and end with deliverables that resemble what they might produce for a client, supervisor, agency, or community partner.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Teaching Role
My role includes developing lab scenarios, writing step-by-step technical instructions, creating supporting videos and examples, designing deliverables, and helping students understand how individual GIS tools fit into a larger analytical process.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Teaching Goals
This approach is designed to help students:

understand GIS as a problem-solving process,
connect technical steps to real-world questions,
evaluate data needs and limitations,
practice complete workflows rather than isolated tools,
produce clear maps, figures, analyses, and written explanations,
build confidence for internships, jobs, research, and community projects.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Instructional Approach
I often frame GIS assignments as realistic scenarios. Students may be asked to support a planning question, analyze environmental conditions, prepare public-facing maps, evaluate spatial patterns, or develop products that communicate results to a specific audience.
The goal is not only to complete a tutorial. The goal is to understand what the workflow is doing, why each step matters, what assumptions are involved, and how the final product should be communicated.

title: Applied GIS Lab Design
text:
Portfolio Significance
This teaching approach connects directly to my broader work in geospatial education, professional development, community-engaged GIS, and student mentoring. It reflects the idea that GIS students need both technical competence and the ability to reason through complex spatial problems.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
The GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination role reflects my service to the Geographic Information Science and Technology program in the Department of Geography and Sustainability at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville.
This service work includes program leadership, student recruitment, course scheduling, curriculum planning, academic and career advising, student-success support, and coordination across faculty, staff, students, alumni, and college advisors.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Program Context
The GIST program prepares students to use spatial data, mapping, modeling, visualization, and geospatial analysis to answer questions and solve problems. Students learn to use GIST as system for combining data management, data science, data visualization, and geography, with applied preparation for work across public, private, nonprofit, and academic settings. Learn more about GIST at UTK.
The program supports several undergraduate pathways, including the Geographic Information Science and Technology BS, the Geography BA with a Geospatial Science and Technology concentration, and the Geographic Information Science minor. The department also offers UAS-related undergraduate programs connected to geospatial technology.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Service Role
I served as GIST Program Director for three years. In that role, I supported the program’s day-to-day function, long-term planning, student recruitment, advising, and curriculum coordination.
This work often happened behind the scenes, but is essential to student success. Students need required courses offered at the right time, clear advising pathways, workable schedules, access to overrides when appropriate, and help connecting their academic choices to career goals.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Key Contributions
My service to the GIST program includes:

serving as GIST Program Director,
organizing and hosting the annual GIST Planning Retreat,
supporting course scheduling and sequencing,
checking for scheduling conflicts among required and elective courses,
finding course solutions for individual students,
managing course overrides,
supporting recruitment across the BS, BA concentration, and GIS minor,
providing academic and career advising,
recruiting students through class visits, college advising events, department tours, and Discover CAS,
helping plan and promote the 4+1 pathway in GIST,
supporting Tech Fee planning and requests,
preparing APR and accreditation-related documentation.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Student Success and Program Demand
One of the accomplishments I am most proud of is the program’s strong post-graduation success. 100% of GIST students who graduate with a Bachelor of Science have successful post-graduation outcomes, including full-time work, graduate school, and post-graduation internships that transition into additional opportunities. View GIST post-graduation outcomes.
The program also has strong demand across multiple pathways: the GIST BS, the Geography BA with GIST concentration, and the GIS minor. That demand creates opportunities for students, but it also requires careful coordination to make sure required courses are available when students need them.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Curriculum Coordination
A major part of this service role is helping the program function as a coherent curriculum rather than a disconnected set of courses. This includes working on course sequencing, anticipating student demand, reviewing historical course offerings, identifying bottlenecks, and helping faculty and staff plan future offerings.
The annual GIST Planning Retreat was created to give faculty and staff a structured space to discuss curriculum, course timing, student needs, program goals, and long-term planning. The retreat helps turn informal conversations into coordinated planning and shared decisions.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Advising and Recruitment
In addition to formal curriculum work, I provide academic and career advising for students interested in GIST. This includes helping students understand course pathways, identify useful electives, consider internships and research opportunities, and connect their technical skills to career goals.
Recruitment work includes speaking with prospective students, participating in college advising and transition events, giving department tours, visiting classes, and helping students understand how GIST can support a wide range of academic and professional interests.

title: GIST Program Leadership and Curriculum Coordination
text:
Service Significance
This service work reflects my commitment to building a strong, student-centered GIST program. Program leadership is not only about curriculum paperwork or course scheduling; it is about creating the conditions that allow students to move through the program successfully and leave with meaningful skills, confidence, and career opportunities.
The work combines administration, advising, recruitment, curriculum planning, student support, and long-term program development. It is one of the central ways I contribute to the department’s teaching mission and to the success of our students.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
The Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement work reflects my service connecting students with alumni, employers, graduate programs, professional mentors, and career-development opportunities.
This work includes organizing the annual Geography and Sustainability Career Mingle, maintaining relationships with alumni, supporting the department alumni network, recruiting professionals to meet with students, and helping students identify internships, jobs, graduate programs, and professional pathways.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
Service Context
Students in Geography, Sustainability, GIST, Urban Planning, and related environmental fields benefit from opportunities to talk directly with professionals. Many students know they are interested in maps, places, sustainability, environmental work, GIS, planning, or community problem-solving, but they may not yet know what those interests look like as careers.
The Career Mingle and related alumni-engagement work are designed to help students build those connections. The goal is to make career exploration more personal, more accessible, and more connected to real people doing relevant work.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
Geography and Sustainability Career Mingle
In partnership with the UTK Center for Career Exploration and Development, I organize, recruit for, and host the annual Geography and Sustainability Career Mingle.
The event is designed as a reverse career fair. Instead of asking students to walk up to employer tables, students meet with professionals in short one-on-one conversations. The focus is on career exploration, professional storytelling, networking, and helping students understand possible pathways after graduation.
The sixth annual Career Mingle included:

111 one-on-one sessions,
50 undergraduate students,
6 graduate students,
students from 20 different majors,
19 employers,
2 graduate schools,
recruiting visits to more than 20 upper-division classes.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
Alumni Engagement
Alumni engagement is a major part of this work. I maintain relationships with alumni to support student opportunities, professional networking, internships, research connections, and post-graduation pathways.
This work includes:

managing the department alumni group on LinkedIn,
organizing informal alumni meet-ups in Knoxville,
helping maintain alumni relationships that support internships and job opportunities,
contributing to the department Alumni Award Ceremony,
nominating alumni for recognition,
supporting monthly Featured Alumni updates on the department website,
inviting alumni and professionals to participate in department events.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
Student Career Support
Beyond the Career Mingle, I regularly share job, internship, fellowship, research, conference, and professional-development opportunities with students. I also support students through advising conversations, reference letters, informal mentoring, and connections to alumni and professional partners.
Recent student-support work has included:

writing reference letters for students and alumni,
sharing job and internship opportunities through student communication channels,
connecting students with professional organizations and conferences,
recruiting students for research and applied GIS opportunities,
coordinating with campus career-development staff,
helping students understand how their GIS, geography, sustainability, and planning skills connect to career pathways.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
Partnership Building
This service work depends on sustained partnerships with alumni, employers, graduate programs, campus career-development staff, faculty, staff, and students.
A major part of the work is identifying professionals who are willing to share their experiences with students, explaining the value of the event, recruiting student participants, and making sure the event is useful for both students and professionals.
The work also supports the department by strengthening relationships with alumni and external partners who can provide internships, research opportunities, guest speakers, field trips, project partnerships, and employment pathways.

title: Career Mingle and Alumni Engagement
text:
Service Significance
The Career Mingle and alumni-engagement work reflect my commitment to helping students see where their education can take them. Career development is not something that happens only at the end of a degree. Students benefit from repeated opportunities to meet professionals, ask questions, hear career stories, and imagine possible futures.
This work also strengthens the department’s professional network. By connecting students, alumni, employers, graduate programs, and campus career-development partners, the Career Mingle helps build a stronger community around Geography, Sustainability, GIST, and related fields.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance EMR StoryMaps
text:
The Tennessee Geographic Alliance EMR StoryMaps are online educational resources developed through volunteer work with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance. These StoryMaps help learners explore electromagnetic radiation, satellite imagery, and remote sensing concepts through real-world environmental examples.
The resources connect abstract physical geography and remote sensing concepts to visible environmental phenomena such as wildfire activity, sea ice, and glaciers.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance EMR StoryMaps
text:

My role included developing online instructional materials, designing StoryMap-based learning experiences, selecting geospatial examples, organizing explanatory content, and creating resources that educators and learners can use to explore remote sensing concepts.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance EMR StoryMaps
text:
Educational Goals
These StoryMaps are designed to help learners:

understand electromagnetic radiation as a foundation for remote sensing,
connect satellite imagery to real environmental patterns and processes,
interpret imagery related to wildfires, sea ice, and glaciers,
explore how different wavelengths and imagery products support geographic analysis,
use web-based geospatial tools to investigate Earth systems.

title: MEGA:BITESS Academy Geospatial Education and Data Hub
text:
The MEGA:BITESS Academy was a multi-year educator professional-development and geospatial outreach project focused on medical entomology, mosquito surveillance, geospatial analysis, science communication, and STEM education.
Through my volunteer work with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance, I supported the geospatial components of the project by helping teachers learn basic GIS skills, collect and use ovitrap data, and share student-generated data through public-facing maps, dashboards, StoryMaps, and a data hub.
The broader MEGA:BITESS Academy was designed to support innovative classroom teaching, build interest in entomology and geospatial sciences, and increase community awareness of La Crosse neuroinvasive disease. The project trained educators to develop STEM projects with middle- and high-school students by examining environmental factors related to mosquito oviposition. The project also emphasized geospatial technology, locally centered maps, science communication, and STEM learning.

title: MEGA:BITESS Academy Geospatial Education and Data Hub
text:
Project Role
My role focused on the geospatial education, data collection, data publishing, and public-facing GIS components of the project.
Responsibilities included:

teaching participating teachers basic GIS concepts and workflows,
developing an introduction to GIS and data visualization for MEGA:BITESS educators,
setting up Survey123 workflows for ovitrap data collection,
cleaning and publishing teacher and student ovitrap data,
building web maps, dashboards, and StoryMaps using project data,
creating and maintaining a MEGA:BITESS data hub to share project data and resources,
developing training videos and online support materials,
helping translate project data into maps and visualizations for teachers, students, and public audiences.

title: MEGA:BITESS Academy Geospatial Education and Data Hub
text:
Geospatial Education Goals
The geospatial components of the project were designed to help teachers and students:

understand how GIS can support public-health and environmental questions,
collect field data using structured digital forms,
explore mosquito surveillance data spatially,
connect local ovitrap observations to broader disease and environmental patterns,
use maps, dashboards, and StoryMaps to communicate scientific information,
build confidence using geospatial tools in classroom and community contexts.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
The Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products page highlights selected geospatial outputs developed as part of a historic cemetery mapping project. These products were designed to support interpretation, documentation, and communication of landscape features that may be difficult to identify from ground-level observation alone.
This work demonstrates how UAV imagery, LiDAR-derived elevation products, cartography, and spatial visualization can support community-engaged projects while also creating meaningful examples for geospatial teaching and student learning.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Geospatial Focus
This portfolio item emphasizes the technical and communication products developed through the project, including:

UAV imagery and orthomosaic products,
LiDAR-derived elevation and surface models,
hillshade and surface visualization products,
map layouts and interpretive cartography,
spatial documentation of visible and subtle landscape features,
visual materials for project communication.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Methods and Workflow
The workflow included field data collection, geospatial data processing, spatial interpretation, map production, and communication with project partners. Products were developed to help translate complex spatial data into useful visual information for interpretation, planning, and discussion.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Cartographic and Communication Goals
The geospatial products were designed to be more than technical outputs. They needed to communicate clearly with audiences who may not regularly work with UAV data, LiDAR, elevation models, or GIS software.
Important design considerations included:

making subtle landscape patterns visible,
choosing appropriate visualizations for elevation and surface features,
balancing technical accuracy with readability,
presenting information in ways that could support discussion with partners and public audiences,
acknowledging limitations in what the data can and cannot show.

title: Westview UAV and LiDAR Geospatial Products
text:
Portfolio Significance
This work is a strong example of applied geospatial communication. It demonstrates how remote sensing, UAV mapping, LiDAR visualization, and cartography can be used together to support a real project with community value.
It also connects directly to my teaching philosophy: GIS products are not just software outputs; they are part of a larger process of asking questions, evaluating data, interpreting results, and communicating clearly.

title: Data Classification in Mapping
text:
The Data Classification in Mapping StoryMap explores how classification choices affect the way mapped data are interpreted.
This project was created as an independent geospatial communication example to examine how different classification methods can emphasize, obscure, or reshape spatial patterns. It highlights an important cartographic idea: maps are not neutral displays of data. Design decisions influence what viewers notice and how they understand geographic patterns.

title: Data Classification in Mapping
text:
Project Role

title: Data Classification in Mapping
text:

The StoryMap was designed to help viewers understand that classification methods are not just technical settings. They are interpretive choices that can change the apparent meaning of a map.
By comparing classification approaches, the project encourages viewers to think critically about how maps represent data and how cartographic choices shape geographic understanding.

title: Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project
text:
The Westview Historic Cemeteries Mapping Project is a community-engaged geospatial project focused on supporting documentation, interpretation, and preservation of historic cemetery landscapes.
The project used geospatial technologies such as UAV imagery, lidar, mapping, and spatial analysis to help identify landscape features, document site conditions, and communicate information about cemetery spaces that can be difficult to interpret from ground-level observation alone.

title: Mentoring & Student Success
text:
My mentoring work focuses on helping students build confidence, develop professional skills, and identify meaningful pathways in GIS, geography, sustainability, remote sensing, UAVs, and related fields. This includes supporting students as they prepare for research opportunities, internships, conferences, graduate school, professional networking, and careers.
I see mentoring as part of geospatial education. Technical skills matter, but students also need help understanding how to talk about their work, connect with professional communities, evaluate opportunities, and imagine possible futures for themselves.

title: Mentoring & Student Success
text:
Mentoring Areas

Undergraduate Research: Supporting students as they develop project ideas, prepare posters and presentations, and share their work with academic and professional audiences.
Conference Preparation: Helping students prepare for events such as TNGIC, ASPRS, SEDAAG, EUReCA, ASUReS, and other professional or academic meetings.
Career Pathways: Helping students explore GIS, planning, environmental, UAV, public-sector, private-sector, nonprofit, and graduate-school options.
Professional Communication: Supporting students as they build resumes, portfolios, abstracts, presentations, posters, and professional introductions.
Community and Professional Connection: Encouraging students to participate in professional organizations, outreach events, applied projects, and networking opportunities.

title: Mentoring & Student Success
text:
Approach
I try to help students see themselves as emerging professionals, not just students completing assignments. This means helping them recognize the value of their work, explain their skills clearly, and connect classroom experiences to real opportunities.
Mentoring often involves translating between academic requirements, professional expectations, and individual student goals. The goal is to help students make informed choices and build confidence through experience.

I think of geospatial products as communication tools, not just technical outputs. A good map or GIS product should be accurate, readable, purposeful, and appropriate for the audience using it.
This means paying attention to data quality, cartographic design, uncertainty, context, accessibility, and the decisions the product is meant to support.

title: Community GIS Projects
text:
My community-engaged GIS work focuses on using geospatial tools to support partner organizations, public-facing projects, applied research, and student learning. These projects often combine mapping, spatial analysis, UAV data collection, remote sensing, cartography, and communication with real-world questions and community needs.
Many of these projects also serve as learning opportunities for students. They provide a way to connect technical GIS skills with professional expectations, partner communication, project management, data limitations, and public-facing deliverables.

title: Community GIS Projects
text:
Project Areas

Historic Preservation and Cultural Landscapes: Mapping and documenting cemeteries, historic sites, and community landscapes.
Environmental and Conservation Mapping: Supporting conservation, land management, trail planning, and natural-resource questions.
Public Infrastructure and Facilities: Using GIS and UAV data to support inspection, documentation, planning, and communication.
Community Partner Support: Developing maps, analyses, and geospatial products for organizations with specific applied needs.
Student-Engaged Applied GIS: Creating opportunities for students to participate in meaningful geospatial projects beyond the classroom.

title: Community GIS Projects
text:
Approach
Community GIS projects require more than technical analysis. A successful project depends on understanding the partner’s question, identifying appropriate data, communicating limitations, and producing deliverables that are useful to the intended audience.
I try to approach these projects as collaborative problem-solving efforts. The goal is not simply to create a map or dataset, but to help partners better understand places, patterns, conditions, and decisions.

title: About
text:
I am a GIS educator, outreach coordinator, and community-engaged geospatial practitioner at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville. My work focuses on helping students, educators, professionals, and community partners use geospatial tools to understand problems, communicate clearly, and make better decisions.
My professional work sits at the intersection of teaching, outreach, applied GIS, uncrewed aerial systems, cartography, and student mentoring. I develop applied learning experiences that connect technical skills with real-world questions, including GIS analysis, web mapping, UAV data collection, remote sensing, cartographic communication, and professional development.

title: About
text:
Professional Focus
My work includes:

Teaching undergraduate and professional-development courses in GIS, cartography, UAVs, remote sensing, and geospatial problem solving.
Developing workshops and outreach programs for students, educators, public agencies, and professional audiences.
Supporting community-engaged GIS and UAV projects with students, colleagues, and partner organizations.
Mentoring students as they prepare for conferences, research opportunities, internships, jobs, and graduate school.
Contributing to professional organizations, outreach initiatives, and geospatial education efforts across Tennessee.

title: About
text:
Approach
I try to design learning experiences around realistic problems rather than isolated tools. In GIS, the technical steps matter, but they are only part of the work. Students and professionals also need to understand the question being asked, the data needed to answer it, the limitations of the analysis, and the best way to communicate results to a real audience.
This portfolio highlights selected examples of that work, including teaching materials, workshops, community projects, geospatial products, mentoring activities, service roles, and professional recognition.

title: Workshops & Outreach
text:
My outreach work focuses on helping students, educators, professionals, public agencies, and community partners use geospatial tools more effectively. These activities often connect GIS, cartography, UAVs, remote sensing, and professional development with real-world needs beyond a traditional classroom setting.
Workshops and outreach events allow me to translate technical geospatial topics into practical learning experiences for different audiences, including undergraduate students, K–12 educators, working professionals, community organizations, and public-sector partners.

title: Workshops & Outreach
text:
Outreach Areas

FAA Part 107 and UAV Education: Workshops and learning materials that help participants understand airspace, regulations, weather, sectional charts, airport operations, and safe UAV operations.
GIS Professional Development: Training opportunities focused on practical GIS workflows, geospatial problem solving, map communication, and applied spatial analysis.
K–12 Educator Support: Professional development activities that help teachers connect geospatial tools, UAVs, maps, and spatial thinking to classroom learning.
Community and Public Outreach: Events and projects that make geospatial technology more accessible to community partners and public audiences.
Student Professional Development: Activities that help students prepare for conferences, internships, research opportunities, portfolios, and careers in GIS and geospatial technology.

title: Workshops & Outreach
text:
Approach
I try to design workshops around what participants actually need to do after the event. Rather than focusing only on software demonstrations, I emphasize practical workflows, decision points, common mistakes, and the reasoning behind technical steps.
This approach is especially important in geospatial outreach because participants often need to apply what they learn in complex, real-world settings. A useful workshop should help people understand not only which buttons to press, but also why the workflow matters, what limitations to watch for, and how to communicate results clearly.

title: Choose Your Truth
text:
The Choose Your Truth StoryMap explores how data choices, visual framing, and map design influence interpretation.
This project was created as an independent geospatial communication example to examine how maps can shape the stories people tell with data. It reflects a central theme in cartography and geovisualization: the same topic can be represented in different ways depending on the data, design choices, framing, and intended message.

title: Choose Your Truth
text:
Project Role
My role included developing the StoryMap, selecting examples, designing the visual narrative, and using geospatial storytelling to encourage critical interpretation of maps and data.

title: Choose Your Truth
text:

This project emphasizes:

map interpretation,
visual framing,
data communication,
cartographic storytelling,
geospatial literacy,
critical thinking about maps and evidence.

title: Choose Your Truth
text:
Communication Goals
The StoryMap was designed to encourage viewers to question how maps and data visualizations are constructed. It asks audiences to think about what is being shown, what is left out, how categories and visual choices affect interpretation, and how maps can support different narratives.

title: MEGA:BITESS Geospatial Data Hub and Mapping Resources
text:
The MEGA:BITESS Geospatial Data Hub and Mapping Resources project highlights the geospatial infrastructure developed to support teacher-collected ovitrap data, mosquito surveillance education, web mapping, public data sharing, and science communication.
As part of my volunteer work with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance, I helped build the GIS and data-sharing components that allowed teachers and students to collect, visualize, analyze, and communicate data connected to the MEGA:BITESS Academy.

title: MEGA:BITESS Geospatial Data Hub and Mapping Resources
text:
Geospatial Role
My role focused on designing and supporting the geospatial data workflow for the project.
This included:

setting up Survey123 workflows for teacher and student ovitrap data collection,
supporting teachers as they learned basic GIS and data visualization skills,
cleaning and preparing ovitrap data for publication,
publishing datasets for use in maps, dashboards, and StoryMaps,
building web maps and dashboards using project data,
creating public-facing StoryMaps and data resources,
developing a MEGA:BITESS data hub to organize and share project data,
creating training videos and support materials for educators.

title: MEGA:BITESS Geospatial Data Hub and Mapping Resources
text:
Data Workflow
The project required a full geospatial data pipeline, from field collection to public communication.
The workflow included:

Field data collection using Survey123 forms for ovitrap observations.
Data review and cleaning to prepare teacher and student-submitted records for mapping and analysis.
Data publishing through ArcGIS Online and the MEGA:BITESS data hub.
Map and dashboard development to visualize ovitrap data and related disease-context datasets.
StoryMap and training-resource development to support interpretation, instruction, and public communication.

MEGA:BITESS data hub,
Survey123 data collection workflows,

StoryMaps,

Communication Goals
The geospatial resources were designed to support both technical and educational communication. Teachers needed usable tools for collecting and exploring data, while students and community audiences needed maps and visualizations that made mosquito surveillance and disease-context information understandable.
The project also connected local field observations to broader public-health and environmental patterns. This helped make geospatial technology part of a larger science communication and STEM education effort.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance Open Data Hub
text:
The Tennessee Geographic Alliance Open Data Hub is an educator-facing geospatial resource site designed to help teachers find and use real data, maps, dashboards, PDFs, and classroom-ready visual resources.
The project was developed in response to a common challenge in K–12 geography and GIS education: many teachers are interested in using real data in the classroom, but they often do not have time to find datasets, prepare them for mapping, build visualizations, or become GIS specialists before using them with students.
The goal of the hub was to lower that barrier by creating more turnkey geospatial resources that teachers could use, adapt, and explore.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance Open Data Hub
text:
Project Role
My role included building and organizing the open data hub, identifying useful datasets, publishing geospatial content, creating maps and dashboards, developing downloadable resources, and organizing materials in ways that would be more accessible to educators.
Responsibilities included:

identifying datasets with potential classroom value,
preparing and publishing geospatial data,
creating pre-built maps, dashboards, documents, and visual resources,
organizing resources through a public-facing data hub,
supporting teacher access to real data without requiring advanced GIS expertise,
developing resources that could be used in workshops, classroom activities, and independent teacher exploration.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance Open Data Hub
text:
Educational Purpose
The hub was designed to help teachers:

find real geospatial data more easily,
use pre-built maps and dashboards instead of starting from scratch,
connect data visualization to geography instruction,
access classroom-ready PDFs, apps, and online resources,
introduce students to real-world data and spatial patterns,
reduce the technical barriers associated with classroom GIS use.

title: Tennessee Geographic Alliance Open Data Hub
text:

This project demonstrates my work in geospatial resource design, open data publishing, web GIS, educator support, and data visualization for non-specialist audiences.
It is also a strong example of my broader outreach philosophy: teachers are more likely to use GIS when resources are accessible, organized, reliable, and connected to real classroom needs. The hub was designed to make geospatial data more usable for educators who may not have the time or technical background to build everything from scratch.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
The Decoding Part 107 Workshop is a professional-development workshop designed to help participants understand the knowledge areas required for safe and legal small uncrewed aircraft systems operations.
The workshop focuses on FAA Part 107 concepts, aviation decision-making, airspace, airport operations, sectional charts, weather, loading and performance, radio communications, and practical preparation for the remote pilot certification exam.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Project Role
My role includes developing workshop materials, organizing instructional content, teaching key aviation and geospatial concepts, building practice resources, and adapting the workshop for different audiences, including educators, students, and geospatial professionals.

title: Decoding Part 107 Workshop
text:
Teaching and Outreach Goals
This workshop is designed to help participants:

understand why FAA Part 107 matters for educational and professional UAV use,
interpret aviation charts and airspace information,
identify weather and operational risk factors,
connect UAV operations to real-world geospatial applications,
build confidence using aviation resources and terminology,
prepare for additional study toward the FAA Remote Pilot Certificate.

title: Mythical Cartography
text:
The Mythical Cartography workshop is a creative geography education project developed through volunteer work with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance. The workshop uses art, imagination, topography, and physical geography concepts to help educators explore cartography and landscape interpretation in an engaging way.
By combining creative expression with geographic thinking, the project helps teachers and students see maps not just as technical products, but as tools for understanding landforms, place, scale, and spatial relationships.
The project was later featured on the National Geographic Education Blog as an example of creative geography education and Explorer Mindset teaching.

title: Mythical Cartography
text:
Project Role
My role included helping develop the workshop, support the geospatial and physical geography components, and create public-facing resources that teachers could use in their own classrooms.
Responsibilities included:

helping design the workshop structure and learning activities,
supporting the use of topography and physical geography concepts,
connecting artistic creation with cartographic and geographic thinking,
helping build public-facing online workshop resources,
contributing to a workshop model that made geography creative, accessible, and visually compelling.

title: Mythical Cartography
text:
Educational Goals
The workshop was designed to help educators:

use art to teach geography and mapping concepts,
introduce topography and landform interpretation in creative ways,
help students think spatially about landscapes and terrain,
connect imagination and storytelling to map design,
build classroom activities that support physical geography and cartography.

title: Mythical Cartography
text:
External Recognition
This workshop was featured on the National Geographic Education Blog in the post Mythical Cartography: A Creative Way to Foster an Explorer Mindset. The post was co-written with Nate Vanderzee and Lauren Sinclair and describes the workshop’s connection to cartography, physical geography, creative mapmaking, and National Geographic’s Explorer Mindset.

title: Mythical Cartography
text:
Portfolio Significance
This project is one of the most distinctive examples of my outreach work. It shows how geospatial education can be creative, interdisciplinary, and highly engaging while still building real geographic understanding.
The project is also a good example of the way I think about teaching and outreach more broadly: the most meaningful learning experiences often connect technical ideas to human creativity, storytelling, and visual communication.

title: TGA Educator Geospatial Workshops
text:
The TGA Educator Geospatial Workshops are a collection of short-format professional-development resources developed through volunteer work with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance. These workshops introduce teachers to geospatial technology, GIS applications, mapping, spatial thinking, and classroom-ready resources for different educational audiences.
The workshops vary in length and format, from one-hour in-service sessions to focused professional-development activities for specific teacher groups. Together, they demonstrate how geospatial tools can be adapted for natural hazards instruction, career and technical education, AP Human Geography, and 4-H programming.

title: TGA Educator Geospatial Workshops
text:

text:

Rather than treating GIS as a standalone technical topic, these workshops connect geospatial tools to real instructional contexts such as natural hazards, human geography, career and technical education, and youth development programming.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
The Campus and Community Partnership Development page highlights service work focused on building connections among students, faculty, staff, alumni, campus units, community partners, and external organizations.
This work often begins with a conversation: a community partner has a need, a faculty member has an idea, a student needs an opportunity, or a campus unit needs technical support. My role is frequently to connect people, clarify the opportunity, identify useful geospatial tools, and help move the idea toward a project, event, class partnership, field experience, or professional-development opportunity.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
Service Context
Geospatial work is highly collaborative. GIS, UAS, remote sensing, and field data collection often require partnerships across departments, organizations, technical experts, community stakeholders, and students.
My partnership-development service focuses on identifying those connections and helping make them productive. This includes supporting community-engaged classes, building campus partnerships, coordinating with professional organizations, connecting faculty with external partners, and helping students access applied experiences.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
Campus Partnerships
I support partnerships across the University of Tennessee campus, especially when geospatial tools, UAS operations, sustainability, student career development, or applied data collection are involved.
Examples include:

establishing a UT System UAV Working Group to address changes in federal legislation, purchasing, and institutional UAS needs,
supporting the UTK Office of Sustainability Green Career Fair,
coordinating with the Center for Career Exploration and Development on career events, Lunch and Learns, job fairs, and student opportunities,
supporting ArcGIS Online administration in partnership with OIT,
coordinating UAS flight permissions at Lakeshore Park for GEOG 314,
connecting faculty with external partners for field trips and applied learning experiences.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
Community-Engaged Course Partnerships
A major part of this service work is finding and supporting partners for GEOG 420: GIS in the Community and other applied geospatial learning opportunities.
Recent partnership work has included:

identifying and recruiting community partners for GIS in the Community projects,
supporting the Costa Rica study abroad partnership with GISetc and community stakeholders in Uvita,
connecting with Bad Elf, TNGIC, and Woolpert to support GEOG 420-related opportunities,
helping coordinate student projects that produce useful data, maps, dashboards, and presentations for partners,
supporting student engagement with real community questions and professional expectations.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
Applied Research and Student Opportunities
This partnership work also helps create applied research and professional-development opportunities for students.
Examples include:

supporting TNView by promoting opportunities through TNGIC,
hosting fellowships and competitions,
recruiting student participants,
supervising undergraduate research projects,
supporting the UTIA Medical Entomology tick-monitoring dashboard and online portal,
supporting student work related to trail access and environmental data projects,
connecting students with conferences, employers, alumni, and professional organizations.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
External and Community Partnerships
I also help develop relationships with external organizations and community partners whose work intersects with GIS, UAS, remote sensing, environmental monitoring, planning, and education.
Examples include:

starting conversations around potential LiDAR projects at Cataloochee and Cades Cove with Great Smoky Mountains National Park contacts,
helping expand those conversations to include faculty, staff, and geospatial partners,
connecting physical geography faculty with a department alumnus at TVA to support River Forecast Center field trip opportunities,
hosting tours and demonstrations for high school students, Tennessee Governor’s School students, and other visitors,
providing career coaching support for local AmeriCorps members affected by changes in federal policy and funding,
supporting Tennessee Geographic Alliance events and planning efforts.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
Role as Connector
A common thread across this work is connection. I help identify who needs to be in the room, what resources are available, what constraints need to be considered, and how a project or partnership can move from idea to implementation.
This work often includes:

stakeholder communication,
partner recruitment,
project scoping,
student recruitment,
equipment and logistics planning,
campus coordination,
outreach,
follow-up communication,
helping translate technical geospatial capacity into useful support for partners.

title: Campus and Community Partnership Development
text:
Service Significance
This service work reflects my commitment to making geospatial education more connected to real people, real places, and real problems. Partnerships give students meaningful applied experiences, help faculty and staff expand their work, support campus and community needs, and strengthen relationships between the department and external organizations.
By helping build and maintain these partnerships, I support a broader geospatial ecosystem that connects teaching, service, research, outreach, career development, and community engagement.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
The Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support page highlights a broad set of department service work that supports visibility, community-building, student opportunities, events, communications, and geospatial teaching infrastructure.
Much of this work happens behind the scenes, but it is essential to the day-to-day function of the Department of Geography and Sustainability. It helps students present their work, faculty and staff communicate with audiences, events run smoothly, and geospatial teaching spaces remain useful for classes, outreach, and demonstrations.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
Service Context
Departments depend on more than formal courses and research projects. They also need events, communications systems, shared equipment, student support, professional visibility, and spaces where faculty, staff, students, alumni, and visitors can connect.
My department service in this area focuses on maintaining and improving those systems. This includes supporting department events, digital and physical communications, geospatial equipment, student poster printing, teaching spaces, and public-facing department visibility.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
Department Events
I help organize, support, and promote several department events each year. These events create opportunities for students, faculty, staff, alumni, and community partners to gather, celebrate accomplishments, and connect with one another.
Recent event-support work includes:

organizing and contributing to the department end-of-year celebration,
helping organize the Alumni Award Ceremony,
recruiting and organizing Lunch and Learn speakers,
supporting Geography Awareness Week speaker recruitment and organizing,
creating event signs and promotional materials,
hosting guest speakers and department visitors,
supporting informal out-of-class community-building events.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
Communications and Visibility
I support department communications through a mix of digital, print, and in-building materials. This work helps keep students informed, highlights faculty and staff, promotes department events, and makes department spaces more welcoming and useful.
This work includes:

maintaining digital signage,
updating the department website,
adding events to the campus event calendar,
updating Featured Alumni content,
creating and designing faculty and staff posters,
updating staff directories on each floor of Burchfiel Geography Building,
updating faculty bulletin boards,
creating signs and graphics for department events,
managing the department alumni LinkedIn group.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
Student Poster and Presentation Support
A major part of my department service is helping students prepare professional-quality posters and visual materials for classes, conferences, research presentations, and outreach events.
This includes maintaining the department plotter, managing printing supplies, troubleshooting print issues, and printing student posters for events such as:

SEDAAG,
AAG,
EUReCA,
AURA,
TNGIC,
class projects,
student research presentations.

This support helps students present their work professionally and lowers barriers for students participating in academic and professional conferences.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
Geospatial Infrastructure Support
I help maintain department geospatial teaching and demonstration infrastructure, including equipment used in classes, labs, outreach, and public demonstrations.
This work includes support for:

the department plotter,
the SimTable,
the Augmented Reality Sandbox,
the Mixed Reality Lab,
GIS lab hardware and software,
teaching equipment used for hands-on class activities,
lab inventory and updates,
software updates and testing.

The SimTable and Augmented Reality Sandbox are used in several hands-on lab activities across the department, helping students and visitors explore terrain, hazards, watersheds, and other geographic processes in interactive ways.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
GIS Lab and Technical Support
I also support the GIS Lab Manager and department geospatial technology needs. This work includes helping develop funding requests, supporting Tech Fee and end-of-fiscal-year equipment planning, assisting with lab hardware and software updates, and helping maintain inventories.
Recent support work has included:

helping prepare funding requests,
supporting lab computer hardware and software updates,
assisting with equipment inventory,
helping rebuild and reorganize spaces after flooding,
updating software,
rewiring hardware,
reassembling desks and tables,
inventorying labs, conference rooms, and storage spaces.

title: Department Events, Communications, and Geospatial Infrastructure Support
text:
Service Significance
This service work reflects my commitment to making the department function well for students, faculty, staff, alumni, and visitors. Some of this work is highly visible, such as events and digital signage. Other parts are less visible, such as maintaining equipment, printing posters, updating software, managing supplies, and troubleshooting technology.
Together, these contributions support the department’s teaching, outreach, research, recruitment, and community-building missions. They help students share their work, help faculty and staff communicate with audiences, and help maintain the geospatial infrastructure needed for hands-on learning.

title: Tennessee ArcGIS Online Mapping Contest
text:
The Tennessee ArcGIS Online Mapping Contest is a statewide mapping contest for middle and high school students. The contest encourages students to use ArcGIS Online, spatial thinking, data, maps, and geographic storytelling to investigate topics connected to Tennessee, their communities, and the world around them.
Through my volunteer work with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance, I help organize and support the contest as a public-facing GIS education initiative for K–12 students and teachers.

title: Tennessee ArcGIS Online Mapping Contest
text:
Project Role
My role includes organizing the contest, recruiting participating teachers, managing the judging process, coordinating sponsors, and publishing the public contest website.
Responsibilities include:

organizing the annual contest structure and timeline,
recruiting teachers and encouraging student participation,
communicating contest requirements and expectations,
managing the judging process,
coordinating judges and judging materials,
working with sponsors and partner organizations,
maintaining and publishing the public contest website,
supporting the visibility of student mapping and GIS education in Tennessee.

title: Tennessee ArcGIS Online Mapping Contest
text:
Contest Goals
The contest is designed to help students:

explore GIS and web mapping through ArcGIS Online,
investigate geographic questions using spatial data,
communicate ideas through maps and storytelling,
develop digital and spatial-literacy skills,
connect classroom learning to real-world geographic issues,
see GIS as a tool for inquiry, communication, and problem solving.

title: Tennessee ArcGIS Online Mapping Contest
text:
Service and Outreach Goals
The contest also supports teachers and the broader GIS education community by:

giving teachers a concrete opportunity to integrate GIS into instruction,
creating a public platform for student work,
connecting K–12 geography education with Tennessee’s geospatial professional community,
strengthening relationships among educators, sponsors, judges, and professional organizations,
increasing awareness of geospatial tools and careers among middle and high school students.
