text:
Portfolio Significance
This project reflects my work in GIS education leadership, K–12 outreach, student engagement, professional service, and public-facing geospatial communication.
It is also a good example of the kind of behind-the-scenes coordination required to make geospatial education programs successful: recruiting participants, building infrastructure, coordinating partners, managing judging, supporting sponsors, and maintaining a clear public presence for the program.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
The TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service page highlights my service to Tennessee’s geospatial professional community through the Tennessee Geographic Information Council, education and outreach work, conference planning, student engagement, and statewide partnerships.
This work reflects my commitment to connecting students, educators, alumni, professionals, employers, and public agencies across Tennessee’s GIS community.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
Professional Service Context
The Tennessee Geographic Information Council plays an important role in connecting GIS professionals, educators, students, vendors, public agencies, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and local governments across the state.
My service with TNGIC has focused on building those connections, supporting professional development, increasing student participation, strengthening education and outreach, and helping the annual conference serve as a meaningful gathering point for Tennessee’s geospatial community.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
TNGIC Leadership
My TNGIC service has included serving on the TNGIC Board of Directors and contributing to conference planning, education and outreach, student engagement, and statewide professional programming.
A major part of this work involves helping create opportunities for students and early-career professionals to participate in the state’s geospatial community. This includes promoting conference participation, connecting students with professionals, supporting competitions and fellowships, and helping students see professional GIS pathways beyond the classroom.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
Annual Conference Planning
I served as Conference Chair for the 2024 and 2025 TNGIC Annual Conferences.
Both conferences reached record attendance, and a significant portion of attendees were either students or alumni from the Department of Geography and Sustainability at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville.
This work included:

conference planning and coordination,
working with the TNGIC Board of Directors,
supporting program development,
recruiting participation,
helping coordinate student involvement,
supporting sponsor and partner relationships,
helping the conference serve both professionals and students.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
Education and Outreach
Education and outreach have been central to my professional service. Through TNGIC and related partnerships, I have worked to support educators, students, and early-career GIS professionals.
This work has included:

promoting student participation in TNGIC,
supporting TNView opportunities through TNGIC,
helping recruit students for fellowships and competitions,
connecting students with professional mentors and employers,
supporting the Tennessee ArcGIS Online Middle and High School Mapping Contest,
maintaining relationships among K–12 educators, university programs, professional GIS organizations, and industry partners.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
Tennessee ArcGIS Online Mapping Contest
The Tennessee ArcGIS Online Middle and High School Mapping Contest is one of the long-running education and outreach efforts connected to this service work. I have hosted the contest annually since 2017 in partnership with the Tennessee Geographic Alliance, TNGIC, and Esri.
When Esri stopped funding the contest in 2024, I helped find replacement support through TNGIC so the contest could continue. This work helps K–12 students and teachers engage with GIS, spatial thinking, and geospatial storytelling.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
Student and Alumni Engagement
A major goal of my TNGIC service is helping students move from the classroom into the professional geospatial community.
This includes:

recruiting students to attend and present at TNGIC,
connecting students with alumni and employers,
helping students understand professional conference expectations,
supporting student research and applied GIS presentations,
promoting opportunities through TNView and other partners,
maintaining relationships with alumni who remain active in Tennessee’s GIS community.

title: TNGIC and Statewide Geospatial Professional Service
text:
Service Significance
This service work reflects my commitment to building a strong, connected geospatial community in Tennessee. TNGIC provides an important bridge between students, educators, government agencies, private companies, nonprofits, and university programs.
My work with TNGIC combines professional leadership, conference planning, education and outreach, student mentoring, and partnership development. It supports not only the organization, but also the broader pipeline of geospatial education and professional development across the state.

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
The Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps StoryMap is an instructor-created example developed for GEOG 311: Introduction to Geovisualization and GIS.
The finished StoryMap was created while testing a lab activity focused on thematic mapping, cartographic design, and visual interpretation. The lab uses county-level presidential election data and demographic data as an applied dataset for comparing different map types and evaluating how design choices influence what readers see.

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Teaching Context
This lab introduces students to common thematic map styles and asks them to think critically about when different approaches are useful, misleading, or inappropriate. Students compare traditional choropleth maps with alternative approaches that can address some of the limitations of county-level election mapping.
The election data provides a familiar and data-rich context, but the purpose of the lab is cartographic rather than political. The emphasis is on map design, data normalization, visual framing, and how thematic maps shape interpretation.

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Guiding Questions
This lab asks students to consider:

What are the most common thematic map styles?
What limitations do these thematic maps have?
When should certain thematic map styles be avoided?
How can a specific thematic map style influence the map reader’s understanding of the data?
How do you build a StoryMap to communicate cartographic findings?

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Project Role
My role included designing the lab activity, selecting and preparing the data sources, testing the workflow, creating the instructor example, and developing a finished StoryMap that demonstrates the type of product students could create through the assignment.
The public StoryMap linked below is my instructor-created example. The underlying lab instructions and instructional datasets are not shared publicly.

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Skills and Tools
This lab supports student learning in:

ArcGIS Online,
ArcGIS StoryMaps,
thematic mapping,
choropleth mapping,
cartograms,
tilegrams,
electoral geography,
county-level data interpretation,
map comparison,
cartographic critique,
visual storytelling.

Students also work with external thematic mapping tools, including:

go-cart.io
Pitch Interactive Tilegrams

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Data Sources
The lab uses public datasets, including:

2020 presidential election results by county from MIT,
2016 and 2020 demographic estimates from NHGIS,
2016 presidential election results by county from Esri.

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Finished Example Product

Exploring 2020 Presidential Election Data with Thematic Maps

title: Exploring Election Data with Thematic Maps
text:
Teaching Significance
This example reflects my approach to teaching cartography as a form of critical communication. Students learn that thematic maps are not neutral containers for data; they are designed arguments shaped by classification, normalization, geography, symbolization, and layout.
By comparing multiple thematic map styles, students can see how the same dataset can support different visual interpretations. The activity helps students move beyond making a technically correct map and toward evaluating whether a map is appropriate, ethical, and understandable for its intended audience.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems is a hands-on course that introduces students to the knowledge, skills, workflows, and professional responsibilities involved in operating small uncrewed aerial systems.
The course is organized around three major components: FAA Part 107 exam preparation, hands-on flight training and data collection, and lab-based geospatial analysis. Students then complete a final project where they apply what they have learned to produce a professional-style UAS product or analysis.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Course Design
The course is designed to help students move from aviation knowledge and operational safety into hands-on flight skills, mission planning, data collection, and geospatial analysis. Students begin by learning the regulatory and safety context for UAS operations, then progress into manual flight, autonomous mission planning, image collection, data processing, and applied project work.
The course also introduces professional and ethical topics that extend beyond the FAA exam, including privacy, insurance, risk management, and responsible data collection.
Students work in teams during hands-on flight practice so they gain experience in multiple operational roles, including pilot and visual observer. Business application presentations and final projects are completed individually.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
FAA Part 107 Exam Preparation
The first major component of the course prepares students for the FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate exam. Students learn the major knowledge areas covered on the exam through lectures, practice activities, quizzes, and full-length practice exams.
Topics include airspace, sectional charts, airport operations, weather, regulations, loading and performance, emergency procedures, crew resource management, and aeronautical decision-making.
Although the course supports Part 107 exam preparation, it also includes topics that are important for professional UAS work but are not emphasized on the exam, such as privacy, insurance, liability, and responsible data collection.
Skills and topics: FAA Part 107 exam preparation, airspace, sectional charts, weather, regulations, airport operations, emergency procedures, aeronautical decision-making, privacy, insurance, professional responsibility.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
section: Hands-On Flight Training and Data Collection
text:
Hands-On Flight Training and Data Collection



Students practicing manual flight controls during UAS field training.


Before handling drones, students complete the FAA TRUST certificate. Once they have completed that safety requirement, they begin hands-on flight training.
The flight training sequence starts with manual flight controls and basic aircraft handling. Students then apply those skills to several applied scenarios, including promotional photography and videography, mission planning for mapping, infrastructure inspection, and thermal data collection.
Students learn to fly several DJI aircraft, including a Phantom 4 Pro, DJI Mavic Mini, and DJI Mavic. The course also includes demonstrations of additional UAS platforms and sensors, including the Inspired Flight 1200A with a thermal sensor.
Hands-on activities include:

manual flight control practice,
pilot and visual observer teamwork,
real estate-style photography,
promotional videography,
mission planning for 2D and 3D mapping,
imagery collection for mapping products,
roof and infrastructure inspection,
thermal imaging for building energy assessment.

Students also learn to use operational planning tools to check weather, NOTAMs, temporary flight restrictions, and other conditions that affect safe UAS operations.
Skills and tools: FAA TRUST, manual flight controls, pilot/visual observer roles, preflight planning, weather checks, NOTAM checks, TFR checks, QGroundControl, mission planning, aerial photography, aerial videography, infrastructure inspection, thermal imaging.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Ground Control and Mapping Workflows



UAS mission planning and data collection activity.


The course introduces students to the relationship between UAS data collection and accurate geospatial products. Students learn how ground control points can improve mapping workflows and gain experience using Emlid GNSS receivers to collect ground control points with RTK.
This component helps students connect field data collection, positional accuracy, photogrammetry, and final mapping outputs.
Skills and tools: Emlid GNSS receivers, RTK, ground control points, field data collection, positional accuracy, mapping workflows.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Lab Analysis and Geospatial Products



Example of UAS imagery processing or mapping output.


The lab portion of the course helps students transform collected imagery into usable geospatial and media products. Students process imagery, evaluate outputs, create mapping products, generate multispectral indices, and edit short promotional videos.
Students use Pix4D to create 2D and 3D mapping products from imagery collected during mission-planning activities. They also use Pix4D to work with multispectral imagery and create vegetation or surface indices. For promotional media workflows, students use DaVinci Resolve to edit short videos from collected footage.
Skills and tools: Pix4D, 2D mapping products, 3D mapping products, photogrammetry, multispectral indices, image processing, geospatial analysis, DaVinci Resolve, promotional video editing.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Business Application Presentation
Students give a short individual presentation on a business application of UAS technology. This assignment asks students to investigate how drones are used in a professional field and explain the value, workflow, benefits, limitations, and potential risks of that application.
This component helps students connect the technical skills from the course to real industries and professional opportunities.
Example application areas: infrastructure inspection, real estate marketing, agriculture, emergency management, construction monitoring, environmental assessment, energy audits, forestry, conservation, and public safety.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Final Student Project
The final project asks students to apply what they have learned across the course. Each student selects a project direction and produces a final deliverable based on the workflow they choose.
Final projects vary by student. Some students create short promotional videos using DaVinci Resolve, while others create 2D and 3D mapping products using Pix4D. Each student gives a short presentation explaining their project, workflow, outputs, and lessons learned.
The project gives students an opportunity to demonstrate the complete UAS workflow: planning a mission, collecting data safely, processing the data, interpreting the results, and communicating their findings.

title: Introduction to Uncrewed Aerial Systems
text:
Teaching Significance
This course reflects my approach to teaching geospatial technology through applied, scaffolded, hands-on learning. Students do not simply learn about drones as a technology; they practice the full workflow required to operate UAS responsibly and produce meaningful outputs.
The course connects aviation knowledge, field operations, mission planning, data collection, RTK ground control, photogrammetry, remote sensing, geospatial analysis, media production, and professional communication. By the end of the semester, students have experience with both the operational and analytical sides of UAS work.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
The Map Projection Poster lab is a cartography-focused assignment developed for GEOG 311: Introduction to Geovisualization and GIS.
In this lab, students create a 24 × 36 inch poster that compares how different map projections change the appearance of spatial data. The activity helps students move beyond memorizing projection terminology and instead visually evaluate how projection choices affect distance, area, shape, direction, and overall map interpretation.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Teaching Context
Map projections are often difficult for students because the concepts can feel abstract. This lab turns projection distortion into a visual comparison exercise. Students work with the same datasets across multiple projections and use poster design to explain how those projections change the appearance of the mapped world.
The assignment also gives students practice creating a large-format cartographic layout in ArcGIS Pro, including maps, tables, explanatory text, and visual organization.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Guiding Questions
This lab asks students to consider:

How do map projections change the appearance of map data?
What map properties are preserved or distorted by common map projections?
What are the common map projection families?
What are some common named map projections?
How can a poster be used to explain cartographic concepts clearly?

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Project Role
My role included designing the lab activity, testing the poster workflow, preparing the instructional structure, and developing the assignment around a finished cartographic deliverable.
The underlying lab instructions and instructional datasets are not shared publicly.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Skills and Tools
This lab supports student learning in:

ArcGIS Pro,
Windows File Explorer,
working with GIS data,
changing map projections,
comparing projection properties,
creating large-format map layouts,
adding tables to ArcGIS Pro layouts,
poster design,
cartographic explanation.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Data Sources
The lab uses data from the Esri Cartography MOOC, including:

latitude and longitude lines,
country boundary polygons,
imaginary circular island polygons,
hurricane tracks.

These layers allow students to compare how different projections affect graticules, global boundaries, circular shapes, and linear features.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Student Deliverable
Students create a 24 × 36 inch map projection poster that visually compares multiple projections and explains how each projection preserves or distorts spatial properties.

title: Map Projection Poster
text:
Teaching Significance
This lab reflects my approach to teaching cartography through visual comparison and applied design. Instead of treating projections as a list of definitions, the activity asks students to observe, compare, explain, and communicate what projection choices do to spatial data.
The poster format also gives students experience producing a polished cartographic communication product. Students must think about layout, hierarchy, explanation, and audience understanding while demonstrating their technical understanding of map projections.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
The Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence is a two-part lab assignment developed for GEOG 311: Introduction to Geovisualization and GIS.
In the first lab, Introduction to Terrain Analysis, students use lidar-derived elevation data to create terrain products for the University of Tennessee, Knoxville campus. In the second lab, Raster Analysis, students use the outputs from the first lab to identify hypothetical locations suitable for solar-panel installation.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Teaching Context
Raster analysis can be challenging for students because it requires them to think differently about GIS data. Instead of working with discrete features such as points, lines, and polygons, students must learn to interpret continuous surfaces, cell values, derived rasters, and map algebra.
This two-part lab sequence is designed to scaffold that transition. Students first learn how elevation data can be transformed into meaningful terrain products. They then use those same products in the following lab to practice reclassification, raster calculator workflows, and suitability-style analysis.
The scenario places students in the role of a sustainability coordinator asked to identify campus locations that may be suitable for solar panels. This gives the technical workflow a practical purpose and helps students connect raster analysis to a real-world planning question.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Analytical Question
The two-part lab sequence is organized around one central question:
What parts of campus are most suitable for solar panels?
Students evaluate potential locations using terrain-derived criteria, including slope, aspect, and visibility from Ayers Hall. The lab introduces the idea that suitability analysis often requires converting several different types of spatial information into comparable criteria, then combining those criteria to identify locations that meet the desired conditions.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Part 1: Introduction to Terrain Analysis
The first lab introduces students to terrain analysis using a 1-meter digital elevation model derived from USGS 3DEP lidar data. Students use ArcGIS Pro to generate several terrain products that describe the physical characteristics of the campus landscape.
Students create an 11 × 17 inch layout containing six small-multiple maps:

DEM,
hillshade,
slope,
aspect,
contours,
viewshed.

These outputs help students understand how a single elevation dataset can be used to produce several different analytical layers.



Screenshot from the terrain analysis layout.


Skills and tools: ArcGIS Pro, empty workspace creation, ZIP extraction, DEM interpretation, slope, aspect, hillshade, contours, viewshed, tool environment settings, metadata review, multi-map layout design.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Part 2: Raster Analysis
The second lab builds directly on the outputs from the terrain analysis lab. Students use the hillshade, aspect, slope, viewshed, and Ayers Hall layers from Part 1 to identify areas that meet the hypothetical solar-panel suitability criteria.
Students reclassify raster layers into binary values of 1 and 0, where 1 represents cells that meet a suitability criterion and 0 represents cells that do not. They then use raster calculator workflows to combine the criteria and identify locations that meet all conditions.
The analysis considers whether locations are:

south-facing,
within an appropriate slope range,
visible from Ayers Hall.

The final output is an 8.5 × 11 inch map layout showing areas around campus that are suitable for solar-panel installation, including the total number of square meters identified as suitable.



Screenshot from the raster analysis solar suitability output.


Skills and tools: ArcGIS Pro, Spatial Analyst, raster reclassification, binary suitability criteria, Raster Calculator, map algebra, cell values, raster overlays, suitability analysis, calculating suitable area.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Project Role
My role included designing the two-part lab sequence, creating the applied campus solar-panel scenario, preparing and testing the terrain and raster workflows, selecting and preparing the data, and structuring the assignments so students could build from one week’s outputs into the next week’s analysis.
The underlying lab instructions and instructional datasets are not shared publicly.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Software
Students use:

ArcGIS Pro,
ArcGIS Spatial Analyst extension,
Windows File Explorer.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Data Sources
The lab sequence uses:

a 1-meter DEM derived from USGS 3DEP lidar data downloaded from The National Map,
a point shapefile representing Ayers Hall,
a polygon shapefile defining the campus analysis area.

The Ayers Hall point layer and campus boundary polygon were created for the lab to support the viewshed analysis and limit the processing extent.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Student Deliverables
Students complete two connected deliverables:

An 11 × 17 inch terrain analysis layout showing the DEM, hillshade, slope, aspect, contours, and viewshed.
An 8.5 × 11 inch raster analysis layout showing campus areas suitable for solar panels, including the total number of square meters identified as suitable.

title: Terrain and Raster Analysis Lab Sequence
text:
Teaching Significance
This lab sequence reflects my approach to teaching GIS as a connected analytical workflow. Students do not simply run terrain tools in isolation. They first create meaningful terrain products from elevation data, then use those products as inputs for a second analysis.
The sequence helps students understand that raster analysis often involves transforming, reclassifying, and combining layers to answer a spatial question. It also gives students a practical introduction to suitability analysis by connecting slope, aspect, visibility, and raster calculator workflows to a realistic campus sustainability scenario.
By the end of the two-part lab, students have practiced both the technical mechanics of raster GIS and the larger analytical process of turning elevation data into evidence for a planning recommendation.

title: Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data
text:
The Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data StoryMap is an instructor-created example developed for GEOG 311: Introduction to Geovisualization and GIS.
The finished StoryMap was created while testing a lab activity titled USPS Dog Bites Risk. The lab uses USPS dog bite data to help students explore how maps, charts, rankings, and narrative explanation can be used to communicate risk patterns across states.

title: Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data
text:
Teaching Context
This teaching example was developed for an introductory geovisualization and GIS course. The activity asks students to examine a real public dataset and consider how geographic patterns can be communicated clearly to a public audience.
The guiding question — which state is the highest risk for letter carriers? — gives students a focused problem that requires them to think about data, normalization, map design, interpretation, and communication.

title: Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data
text:
Project Role
My role included designing the lab activity, testing the full workflow, creating the instructor example, and developing a finished StoryMap that demonstrates the type of product students could create through the assignment.
The public StoryMap linked below is my instructor-created example. The underlying lab instructions and instructional datasets are not shared publicly.

title: Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data
text:
Skills and Concepts
This lab supports student learning in:

StoryMap design,
public data interpretation,
geovisualization,
risk communication,
thematic mapping,
comparing places using spatial data,
developing a clear geographic argument,
translating data into a public-facing narrative.

title: Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data
text:
Finished Example Product

Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data

title: Exploring USPS Dog Bite Data
text:
Teaching Significance
This example reflects my approach to teaching GIS through focused, real-world questions. Students are not simply making a map; they are using spatial data and visual communication to investigate a practical question and explain their findings.
The activity also helps students think critically about how “highest risk” should be measured, how data choices affect interpretation, and how maps and narrative text can work together to communicate results.

title: Service & Leadership
text:
Service has been a central part of my professional career. I believe that geospatial education is strengthened through collaboration, professional engagement, and community involvement. My service activities focus on expanding opportunities for students, supporting the geospatial profession, strengthening educational programs, and connecting universities with practitioners, educators, and community partners.
These leadership roles have included departmental service, statewide professional organizations, conference planning, outreach initiatives, strategic planning, and collaborative projects that extend beyond the classroom.

title: Service & Leadership
text:
Areas of Service

Professional Organizations: Leadership and committee service supporting the geospatial profession and geospatial education.
Conference Planning: Organizing conferences, workshops, and educational programming that connect students, educators, professionals, and community partners.
Program Development: Creating new educational opportunities, outreach programs, workshops, and student experiences.
Department and University Service: Supporting curriculum development, student engagement, outreach, recruitment, and program growth.
Community Engagement: Building partnerships that connect university resources with public agencies, nonprofit organizations, educators, and local communities.

title: Service & Leadership
text:
Philosophy of Service
I view service as an extension of teaching. Bringing people together, creating opportunities, sharing resources, and helping organizations solve problems all contribute to strengthening the geospatial community.
Many of the most rewarding projects I have worked on have resulted from long-term relationships between universities, professional organizations, educators, students, and community partners.
